coredata.dc:title: Controllable synthesis of green and blue fluorescent carbon nanodots for pH and Cu2+ sensing in living cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Lihong
Li, Yanyan
Li, Xiaofeng
Zhao, Bo
Wen, Xiangping
Zhang, Guomei
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: We report a controllable strategy for fabrication of green and blue fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs), and demonstrate their applications for pH and Cu2+ sensing in living cells. Green and blue fluorescent CDs have been synthesized by hydrothermal method and pyrolysis of leeks, respectively, providing an easy way for the production of CDs without the request of tedious synthetic methodology or the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. Green fluorescent CDs (G-CDs) exhibit high tolerance to pH values and external cations. Blue fluorescent CDs (B-CDs) can be applied to pH and Cu2+ sensing. The linear range of Cu2+ detection is 0.01–10.00μM and the detection limit is 0.05μM. For pH detection, there is a good linearity in the pH range of 3.5–10.0. The linear and rapid response of B-CDs to Cu2+ and pH is valuable for Cu2+ and pH sensing in living cells. Confocal fluorescent imaging of human cervical carcinoma cells indicates that B-CDs could visualize Cu2+ and pH fluctuations in living cells with negligible autofluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon nanodots
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The response of CDs to pH values
The response of CDs to metal ions
pH- and Cu2+-dependent intracellular fluorescence imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of CDs from leeks, along with photographs of the corresponding samples under 365nm UV lamp (top) and 470nm excitation (bottom).

Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with B-CDs for 0.5h at pH 10.0 (a), 7.4 (b) and 3.5 (c), respectively. (d) Bright-field image of a; (e) bright-field image of b; (f) bright-field image of c; (g) merged image of a and d; (h) merged image of b and e; (i) merged image of c and f. Excitation wavelength was 405nm, and emission was collected in the blue channel (425–475nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Confocal fluorescence image of HeLa cells without Cu2+. Dynamic fluorescence imaging of Hela cells with Cu2+: (b–h) Images in blue channel; (i) temporal profile of average fluorescence intensity in regions of interest 1 shown in panel b–h. Excitation wavelength was 405nm, and emission was collected in the blue channel (425–475nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Controllable synthesis of green and blue fluorescent carbon nanodots for pH and Cu2+ sensing in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Taiyuan University, Environmental Engineering Department, Taiyuan 030032, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a controllable strategy for fabrication of green and blue fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs), and demonstrate their applications for pH and Cu2+ sensing in living cells. Green and blue fluorescent CDs have been synthesized by hydrothermal method and pyrolysis of leeks, respectively, providing an easy way for the production of CDs without the request of tedious synthetic methodology or the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. Green fluorescent CDs (G-CDs) exhibit high tolerance to pH values and external cations. Blue fluorescent CDs (B-CDs) can be applied to pH and Cu2+ sensing. The linear range of Cu2+ detection is 0.01–10.00μM and the detection limit is 0.05μM. For pH detection, there is a good linearity in the pH range of 3.5–10.0. The linear and rapid response of B-CDs to Cu2+ and pH is valuable for Cu2+ and pH sensing in living cells. Confocal fluorescent imaging of human cervical carcinoma cells indicates that B-CDs could visualize Cu2+ and pH fluctuations in living cells with negligible autofluorescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDs), fascinating fluorescent nanomaterials, were first obtained during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 (Shi et al., 2015a). By virtue of their low toxicity, high aqueous solubility, low photobleaching, non-blinking, and excellent biocompatibility, fluorescent CDs have great promise for a variety of practical applications, such as bioimaging (Shi et al., 2015b), chemical sensor and biosensor (Hou et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2014), drug delivery (Zhang et al., 2015), gene delivery (Liu et al., 2012), dye sensitizers (Gong et al., 2015), catalysis (Li et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013), fluorescent ink (Qu et al., 2012), and mimetics peroxidase (Shi et al., 2011).

During the past few years, many attempts have been made toward lowering the cost, simplifying operation, improving quantum yield (QY), or realizing larger-scale production of CDs. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, tuning the photoluminescence (PL) behavior of CDs has been less explored. Tunable PL emissions is required in order to ensure good performance in light emitting diodes and bioimaging applications. Liu et al. (2015) developed fluorescent CDs with tunable wavelength from 464 to 556nm by introducing glutaraldehyde into the poly (ethylenimine) solution under microwave irradiation and demonstrated their application for effective fluorescent probes in the cell imaging. Bao et al. (2015) synthesized tunable fluorescent CDs from 430 to 610nm by oxidation of carbon fiber in nitric acid and then ultrafiltration and revealed that the PL properties of CDs, mainly influenced by their size and degree of surface oxidation, can be tuned by varying reaction time, temperature, and concentration of nitric acid. Guo et al. (2014) prepared goldenrod and green–yellow fluorescent CDs by hydrothermal decomposition and engineered CD@CD nanoparticles to result in gold fluorescent CDs. Jiang et al. (2015) reported red, green, and blue fluorescent CDs by solvothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine (pPD), o-phenylenediamine (oPD), and m-phenylenediamine (mPD), respectively, and exhibited their excellent multicolor cellular imaging capability under single wavelength excitation. Hu et al. (2015) revealed for the first time an approach to shift PL peaks of CDs to cover the entire visible spectrum (400–710nm) by controlling the dehydration reaction occurring during synthesis. Nevertheless, all these methods suffer from some drawbacks like the requirement of tedious synthetic methodology or the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. Therefore, the development of facile, green and controllable synthesis of color-switchable CDs is still highly desirable.

In the present work, green and blue fluorescent CDs have been synthesized by hydrothermal treatment and pyrolysis of leeks, respectively, for the first time. Green fluorescent CDs (G-CDs) demonstrate high tolerance to pH values and external cations while blue fluorescent CDs (B-CDs) can be used to pH and Cu2+ sensing. Moreover, due to minimum toxicity as well as good PL properties, B-CDs could image pH and Cu2+ fluctuations in living cells.
The experimental section are described in detail in the Supplementary Materials.

B-CDs and G-CDs were fabricated successfully using leeks as the starting materials via one-step pyrolysis and hydrothermal treatment, respectively ( Fig. 1). Hydrothermal synthesis involves reactions in a closed system under mild temperatures (120–280°C) and self-generated pressure, which avoids traditional harsh conditions such as strong acids, high temperatures, and long reaction times (Wu et al., 2015). Pyrolysis can be described as the direct thermal decomposition of biomass in the absence of oxygen (Uslu et al., 2008), which has numerous merits, such as simple experimental setup, easy control of the reaction, and facile operation. Leeks contain carbohydrates, protein, and dietary fiber, which are widespread, easily available, low-cost, and eco-friendly. The preparation of these CDs has been conducted in triplicate and the results are almost the same, which indicates good reproducibility of the products. Both B-CDs and G-CDs freely disperse in water with transparent appearance. To get good PL properties, we investigated the influence of reaction temperature and time on PL properties of the resultant CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the optimum reaction condition for B-CDs is 350°C for 3h and that for G-CDs is 250°C for 3h.
The characterization of CDs are described in detail in the Supplementary Materials.

The optical properties of both B-CDs and G-CDs are displayed in Fig. S4. Both B-CDs and G-CDs show broad absorption from 250 to 400nm. B-CDs show a strong PL at 450nm excited at 360nm while G-CDs display an obvious PL at 520nm excited at 470nm. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the QY of B-CDs is determined to be 5.9%. Using Rhodamin 6G as a reference, the QY of G-CDs is determined to be 5.6%. As shown in the insets of Fig. S4, B-CDs emit blue PL under 365nm excitation and G-CDs emit green PL under 470nm excitation.

To further explore the optical properties of CDs, the PL spectra of both B-CDs and G-CDs under different excitation wavelengths were measured (Fig. S5a and b). With increasing excitation wavelength, the PL intensity of both B-CDs and G-CDs first increases and then decreases, and the PL peak of both B-CDs and G-CDs shows a red shift (the inset in Fig. S5a and b), indicating that both PL intensity and peak are dependent on excitation.

The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of CDs is assessed by fluorescent decay curves. As seen in Fig. S6, the fluorescent decay curve of B-CDs are recorded at 450nm under 360nm excitation and that of G-CDs are recorded at 520nm under 470nm excitation. The decay curves are fitted using exponential functions Y(t) based on a non-linear least squares analysis using the following equation: Y(t)=A 1exp(−t 1/τ 1)+A 2exp(−t 2/τ 2), where A 1 and A 2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ 1 and τ 2. We calculated the average lifetime of B-CDs and G-CDs to be 7.2339ns and 6.6215ns, respectively.

The stability of both B-CDs and G-CDs under various conditions has also been studied. Both B-CDs and G-CDs have excellent stability that their PL intensity do not change even after continuous excitation for three hours (Fig. S7a and c) and storage for three months (Fig. S7b and d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of CDs to pH values
ce:section.ce:para: We studied the response of both B-CDs and G-CDs to pH values for a wide range of pH values. With increasing pH values from 3.0 to 11.0, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs at 450nm enhances significantly under 360nm excitation (Fig. S8a), illustrating that B-CDs have strong pH-dependent behavior. Distinctly, there is a good linearity between PL intensity and pH in the range of 3.5–10.0, according to the linear equation: I=836.6−59.0 pH, R 2=0.9960. The result indicates that B-CDs could be used as fluorescent pH nanosensor within the range of 3.5–10.0. This response of B-CDs to pH values can be attributed to the deprotonation of carboxyl groups (Fig. S8b), which leads to the formation of delocalized π bond and the increase of n electrons. The reversibility is extremely important for pH probe. Fig. S8c shows the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs at 450nm when the pH is switched between 3.0 and 11.0 for four times. After four cycles, the intensity remains almost unchanged. The reproducibility of the emission intensity of pH-sensitive B-CDs clearly demonstrates the excellent reversibility of this CD-based pH sensor. Compared with B-CDs, the PL intensity of G-CDs at 520nm is pH independent over a wide range (4.0–11.0) under 470nm excitation (Fig. S8d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of CDs to metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: We also studied the influence of various external cations on the PL intensity of both B-CDs and G-CDs under pH 7.4 condition, including Co2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Bi3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, K+, Al3+, Hg2+, and Na+. As shown in Fig. S9a, it can be seen that a much lower fluorescence is observed for B-CDs upon addition of Cu2+ ions while other metal ions show either no or slight change in the PL intensity relative to the free B-CDs. This result reveals that B-CDs are highly selective toward Cu2+ ions over the other metal ions and could be developed as an efficient fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ ions. As shown in Fig. S9b, the Cu2+ ions sensing capacity of B-CDs was investigated in the range of 1.0×10−7 M to 1.0×10−4 M under pH 7.4 condition at 360nm excitation. With increasing Cu2+ ions concentration, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs decreases progressively, revealing that the sensing system is sensitive to Cu2+. As shown in Fig. S9c, in low concentration, it fits the linear Stern–Volmer equation with the Ksv constant of 0.048μM−1 (adj. R 2=0.9866), illustrating a quencher inhibits either a photochemical reaction or a photophysical process by a single reaction (dynamic quenching process). Based on the results, the detection limit for Cu2+ is estimated to be 0.05μM, which is much lower than the maximum level (∼20μM) of Cu2+ in drinking water permitted by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (Zhang et al., 2014). With the further increase of the concentration of Cu2+ ions, it does not fit the dynamic quenching process any more. These results indicate that both dynamic and static quenching process may occur at high concentration (Zhao and Swager, 2005). Fluorescence quenching is usually divided into dynamic and static quenching. For dynamic quenching, the interaction is taken place between the quencher and excited state of the fluorescence material, resulting in the decrease in intensity of the fluorescence molecule. However, the absorption of the fluorescence material remains unchanged under dynamic quenching condition. Static quenching takes place between the quencher and fluorescence substance in the ground state, thus generating lightless complexes with a concomitant decrease in fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, the absorption of the fluorescence material changes under static quenching condition. The absorption of B-CDs at various concentrations of Cu2+ ions is in good agreement with the above-mentioned mechanism (Fig. S9d). As shown in Fig. S9e, the PL intensity of G-CDs remains constant even the concentration of external cations reaches up to 100μM except that Cu2+ decreases the PL intensity a little.

To explore the potential application of CDs in living cell, the cytotoxicity of B-CDs was evaluated by the MTT assay in Hela cells. In the MTT assay, MTT could be reduced by the active cellular enzymes in the cells to insoluble blueviolet formazan crystals. The quantitative information about the cytotoxicity of B-CDs can be obtained by measuring the absorption on the cells. We performed the MTT assay in Hela cells with concentrations ranging from 0.1mg/mL to 0.8mg/mL. The results demonstrate that more than 86% of cells are viable (Fig. S10), showing the low toxicity of B-CDs to cultured cells under the experimental conditions at a concentration of 0.6mg/mL. B-CDs were internalized into the cells possibly by endocytosis mechanism (Alfonso et al., 2013; Bao et al., 2015; Vedamalai et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH- and Cu2+-dependent intracellular fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that H+ and Cu2+ are very important in metabolic processes, being indispensable for all living systems. Abnormal pH values can cause cardiopulmonary and neurologic problems (e.g., cancer and Alzheimer's disease) (Izumi et al., 2003). High intracellular Cu2+ concentrations may be toxic to organisms (Alfonso et al., 2013). Therefore, quantitative determination of H+ and Cu2+ is crucial in clinical medicine. Laser confocal imaging can provide time and space resolution for Cu2+ and pH fluctuations in living cells, which offers important information for clinical diagnosis.

We used HeLa cells as a model to study the pH-dependent fluorescence imaging of B-CDs using a confocal fluorescence microscope. HeLa cells were incubated with B-CDs (0.6mg/mL) for 0.5h at 37°C and then with nigericin, which can equilibrate the intracellular and extracellular pH, in three different PBS (pH=3.5, 7.4 and 10.0). As shown in Fig. 2, B-CDs with blue fluorescence emission disperse well within living cells, indicating excellent cell membrane permeability. Furthermore, B-CDs-stained HeLa cells exhibited strong blue fluorescence in PBS at pH 10.0 (Fig. 2a), whereas the fluorescence brightness obviously reduced as the pH was decreased (Fig. 2b and c). Bright-field images after B-CDs incubation confirm the viability of the cells (Fig. 2d–f).Our results demonstrate that B-CDs can be potentially useful for imaging pH in living cells under a confocal fluorescence microscope.

We used HeLa cells as a model to study the Cu2+-dependent fluorescence imaging of B-CDs using a confocal fluorescence microscope. HeLa cells were incubated with B-CDs (0.6mg/mL) for 0.5h at 37°C, and then with Cu2+ (100μM) for 1min. Dynamic fluorescence imaging is performed to observe quenching process in real time by confocal fluorescence microscope. As a control, we also gave confocal fluorescence image of the B-CDs-stained HeLa cells at 60s without Cu2+ and found that the HeLa cells exhibited strong blue fluorescence ( Fig. 3a). After Cu2+ ions are added the fluorescence brightness decreases gradually with extended time (Fig. 3b–h). The fluorescence intensity is calculated by commercial software (Fig. 3i). Our results show that B-CDs can be used potentially to image Cu2+ in living cells under confocal fluorescence microscope.

We have described a green, easy, and economic route for fabricating green and blue fluorescent CDs controllably with the use of ubiquitous and natural leeks as the precursor via one-step hydrothermal treatment and pyrolysis, respectively. Interestingly, B-CDs have been developed as fluorescent nanosensors for rapid, precise and quantitative sensing of pH values and Cu2+ ions. More importantly, we have applied B-CDs as pH and Cu2+ probes in living cells, which may expand significant utilization of B-CDs in biosensing. This strategy may offer a new approach for developing eco-friendly and sensitive sensors in biological and environmental fields. In the future, we expect the advent of more facile and robust synthetic routes and creative applications to better realize the potential of the increasingly important CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475080, 21306108 and 21575084), Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province of China (2013011009-5 and 2014011016-2), and Shanxi Council Scholarship of China, Innovative Talents in Higher School Support Plan (2014107), and Technology Foundation for Selected Overseas Chinese Scholar (grant number 2014-95) in Shanxi Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.10.031.
